[PDF_PAGE 32 | score=9] player character dying, the DM should try to lnake those characﾂｭ ters' deaths play an important part in his story. If it becomes obvious to both the OM and the players that one or more PCs are about to be killed, gi ve the characters a chance to make their deaths meaningful. Imﾂｭ provise a scenario that gives the characters a glorious and memoraﾂｭ ble death scene. For inspiration, consider those war movies and action films where a mortally wounded character remains beﾂｭ hind to take out the pursuing bad guys, or where one character leads the villains astray, knowing it means his own death. The DM might wish to let the characters ignore their fatal wounds for a few rounds while they hack berserk
[PDF_PAGE 29 | score=5] Notice how the second descripﾂｭ tion focuses on what the characﾂｭ ters see and feel. If the DM elnphasizes the sudden movement of the eyes and punctuates it with a sharp sound, he may catch the players off guard. Like their characters, they too have been caught off guard and now share the character's apprehensions. The key to making encounters dramatic is to focus on what makes them fantastic: 窶｢ Stress the fantastic or the unuﾂｭ sual in every encounter. Try to make each encounter unique in SOine way, even if the characﾂｭ ters are encountering SOlneﾂｭ thing they already know about. Make the foes seem unbeatﾂｭ able. Their numbers or apparﾂｭ ent abilities make the chance of failure and death an all-to
[PDF_PAGE 91 | score=2] it), it's an excellent practice for the DM to predetennine a number of encounters before beginning play. This gives him a chance to set up the statistics and motivations for monsters or NPCs encountered. It also allows the OM to answer quesﾂｭ tions like, "What kind of goods is that caravan carrying and where is it headed?tI or tlWhat's the ores' tribe?" Encounters and events develop the stoty line as long as they bear some relationship to the stOIyS priﾂｭ mary villain or adversary. Battles with the foe's evil minions often bring insight into the complexity of the plot. Provide a balanced mix combat and encounters. Remember to add in plot twists as necessary to deepen (or conﾂｭ fuse) the plot, a
[PDF_PAGE 7 | score=1] Logistics of Play buys the farm (dies). Don't play fa- voritism games. Accept a charac- ter's death' calmly, don't belabor it. The DM is human (really, it's true!) and will make mistakes. If a mis- take is fatal, ask the DM to change his decision in good grace, without recriminations. Be sure to compli- ment the DM on a good game! Be Kind to the Players Be Fair. Assume they are not cheating if they haven't been caught at it. Let the players make their own decisions. Don't manip- ulate their characters as if they were part of a navel or railroad them into choosing predetermined paths. Don't punish them for be- ing clever. If they avoid or foil the best trap in the dungeon, then re- ward them 
[PDF_PAGE 16 | score=1] als ahead of time. Know where the players are and what they were doﾂｭ ing last game, be familiar with their foes and up-to-date on the status of the world. As stated previously, advance reﾂｭ view is especially important with purchased or borrowed scenarios. When rulU1ing a game that is not of his own creation, the OM must spend enough time to become thoroughly familiar with its deﾂｭ tails. When a OM creates his own world and adventures, he usually has more background information in mind than he actually writes down. With a purchased or borﾂｭ rowed adventure, this is not the case. The information is present, but may be in such abundance that important details can be overﾂｭ looked. Make notes in th
[PDF_PAGE 25 | score=1] ers. Of course, excessive talking is likely to bring wandering monﾂｭ sters to the area to investigate, so make some of the rolls bring actual monsters. This will keep the playﾂｭ ers on their toes in the future, espeﾂｭ cially if the OM wiped out half the party the last time they were not paying attention. Messy Maps Exact room dimensions are anﾂｭ other type of infonnation that DMs should not give out freely. How many characters adventure with a yard stick in hand? Take a good look at the players maps. If they are as precise as the DMs, then he must presume that the players are either professional surveyors or, more likely, that he is giving out way to much dimensional inforﾂｭ mation. The best that
[PDF_PAGE 27 | score=1] covering the nature of their unﾂｭ known foe than they are with learning how much treasure it is hiding. Keep the players guessing, keep them on edge, even make them afraid-they'll love you for it. Funny-Ha-Ha Humor, in all its various fonns can break the tension, relieve the fear, and proﾂｭ vide a balance for all the intensity associated with cOInbat and life-orﾂｭ death puzzle-solving. A joke (even outside the context of the game), a fwmy incident, or a silly NPC porﾂｭ trayal can slow the pace of the game down and give the players enough time to regain their perﾂｭ spective on a situation. Humor can also provide a deadly trap. Picture an inept NPC, someﾂｭ one who could be dangerous if he had his wi
